audits.summary: The single-visit experience (capture → analysis → save) is polished, but the core differentiating journey — a practitioner adding a second visit to a patient and running a comparison — is broken: "Add visit" from the patient screen never binds the new case to that patient, so it lands in the Unassigned bucket where it can never be compared against the first visit, and the promised re-bind capability does not exist. Several practitioner-trust features are advertised but inert (Face ID lock, archive recovery, re-binding), and the flagship comparison view both hides behind a multi-select menu and renders surface-displacement deltas with wrong units. The Patients/Capture tab split is the structural root cause: Capture is patient-agnostic by design, which works for ad-hoc demos but contradicts the longitudinal-tracking value proposition that differentiates FaceMap from QOVES.

findings.title: "Add visit" from a patient never attaches the visit to that patient — breaks the second-visit comparison journey
findings.detail: PatientDetailScreen.swift:89-98 pushes a bare CaptureScreen() with a comment "binding handled in CaptureScreen save" that is false: CaptureScreen takes no patient and pushes AnalysisScreen(multiPose:) with no existingCase (CaptureScreen.swift:69-73). The save sheet (AnalysisScreen.swift:442-459) creates a PatientCase with patient == nil, and CaseStore.save (CaseStore.swift:80-84) silently reassigns it to the "Unassigned" bucket. Since ComparisonScreen is only reachable via multi-select inside one patient's detail screen, a second visit captured this way can never be compared with the first — the app's headline value prop (visit-over-visit outcome tracking) dead-ends on first real use.
findings.recommendation: Thread the patient through the flow: give CaptureScreen and AnalysisScreen an optional `patient: Patient?` parameter; when present, the save sheet pre-fills/locks the patient, auto-suggests label "Visit N", and sets pc.patient before store.save. PatientDetailScreen passes `patient` from "Add visit".

findings.title: Promised "re-bind later" for Unassigned cases does not exist anywhere in the UI
findings.detail: The empty-state copy (PatientListScreen.swift:85) tells practitioners "Cases captured ad-hoc go into an Unassigned bucket you can re-bind later", but no screen offers a move/re-assign action: PatientDetailScreen's visit rows and AnalysisScreen's toolbar have no "Move to patient" option, and CaseStore has no reassign method. Every ad-hoc capture from the Capture tab is permanently stranded in Unassigned, which compounds the Add-visit binding bug — there is no recovery path even manually.
findings.recommendation: Add a "Move to patient…" action (context menu on visit rows in PatientDetailScreen and a toolbar item in AnalysisScreen when existingCase != nil) presenting a patient picker plus "New patient" inline; implement CaseStore.reassign(_:to:).

findings.title: Capture session state never resets — a second capture session can silently mix poses from two different visits
findings.detail: CaptureScreen's @State (captures, photos, currentPose) is never cleared after a session completes; there is no reset in handleSnapshot after building the MultiPoseCapture (CaptureScreen.swift:78-96), no .onAppear reset, and no "start over"/per-pose retake control anywhere in the flow. Because the Capture tab's NavigationStack keeps the view alive, returning later leaves currentPose at .obliqueR with stale frontal/obliqueL captures; the next snapshot replaces only obliqueR and immediately pushes AnalysisScreen with a frontal mesh from the previous patient — a clinically dangerous cross-patient data mix. There is also no way to retake a single botched pose mid-flow without finishing all three.
findings.recommendation: Reset all capture state when a MultiPoseCapture is finalized (or on .onAppear when all poses are filled), and add a "Restart" button plus per-pose retake (tap a completed segment in the progress bar to recapture that pose).

findings.title: Face ID lock setting is a no-op — patient data is not actually protected
findings.detail: The "Lock with Face ID" toggle (SettingsScreen.swift:7,22-36) promises "FaceMap re-authenticates with Face ID before opening saved cases", but `biometricLockEnabled` is read nowhere outside SettingsScreen — FaceMapApp.swift and DisclaimerGate.swift never evaluate it. A practitioner storing facial meshes and clinical photos of patients on a shared clinic iPad believes the data is biometrically protected when it is not. For a medical-adjacent product this is a trust and likely compliance problem.
findings.recommendation: Enforce the setting: wrap ContentView (inside DisclaimerGate) in a lock view that calls LAContext.evaluatePolicy on launch and on scenePhase .active when biometricLockEnabled is true, blurring content until authenticated.

findings.title: Comparison delta table shows surface displacement, expression, and skin metrics with wrong units
findings.detail: ComparisonScreen.formatValue (ComparisonScreen.swift:329-336) only special-cases canthal tilt and asymmetry; SurfaceDisplacementMetric (value in meters), ExpressionAsymmetryMetric (unitless ratio), and SkinQualityAnalyzer (texture index) all fall through to the default "%.1f%%" branch which multiplies by 100 — a 2 mm projection deficit renders as "0.2%" in the flagship before/after view. AnalysisScreen formats the same metrics correctly (AnalysisScreen.swift:548-556), so visit detail and comparison contradict each other, undermining clinical credibility exactly where the practitioner is judging outcomes.
findings.recommendation: Extract the metric-formatting switch from AnalysisScreen into a shared MetricValueFormatter used by both screens, covering all 8 metric ids.

findings.title: Archiving is one-way: no archived-patients list, no undo, no delete-patient UI
findings.detail: Swipe-to-delete archives a patient (PatientListScreen.swift:32-34) and the detail menu offers "Archive patient" (PatientDetailScreen.swift:55-57) with no confirmation; the list query filters archivedAt == nil (PatientListScreen.swift:9), and CaseStore.unarchive / deletePatient (CaseStore.swift:67-76) are never called from any view. An accidental swipe makes a patient's entire visit history vanish with no recovery path and no permanent-delete path either (relevant for data-retention requests).
findings.recommendation: Add an "Archived" section or filter in PatientListScreen with Unarchive and Delete (with confirmation) actions, plus a confirmation dialog on archive itself.

findings.title: No backup or data-export path: device loss destroys every patient record
findings.detail: The SwiftData store is deliberately excluded from iCloud/Finder backups (FaceMapApp.swift:33-48), cloud sync is a disabled stub (SettingsScreen.swift:81-89), and there is no export/import of the case database. A practitioner who builds up months of longitudinal data — the product's whole differentiation — loses everything if the phone is lost, broken, or replaced. This is the largest adoption blocker for the target buyer and deserves an interim answer before Phase 5 sync.
findings.recommendation: Add an encrypted local export/import (password-protected archive of the store via ShareSheet, restore via file importer) under Settings as a stopgap until cloud sync ships.

findings.title: Visit comparison is undiscoverable: hidden behind an ellipsis menu plus a multi-select ritual
findings.detail: Reaching ComparisonScreen requires: open patient → tap ellipsis → "Compare visits" → enter multi-select → check exactly two visits → tap a small "Compare 2/2" link (PatientDetailScreen.swift:48-53, 232-239). The most common case — latest vs. previous visit — takes six taps and is invisible to a first-time user. For the feature that differentiates FaceMap from 2D competitors, there is no "Compare with previous visit" affordance on the latest-visit card, on visit rows, or after saving a second visit.
findings.recommendation: Add a one-tap "Compare with previous" button on the latest-visit card (enabled when ≥2 visits exist) and a swipe/context-menu action on visit rows; keep multi-select for arbitrary pairs.

findings.title: No exportable before/after report — comparison results are trapped on screen
findings.detail: PDF export exists only on AnalysisScreen for a single saved visit (AnalysisScreen.swift:380-387, 510-519), and TreatmentPlanPDF is already stale (predates photos, region changes, skin metric). ComparisonScreen — containing the before/after photos, region projection changes, and metric deltas a practitioner would actually show a patient or keep in the chart — has no share/export at all. The before/after artifact is the single most valuable deliverable in aesthetic-medicine workflows.
findings.recommendation: Add a ComparisonReportPDF (side-by-side photos, region changes table, metric deltas) with a share button in ComparisonScreen's toolbar; fold the TreatmentPlanPDF refresh (photos, skin metric, all 8 metrics) into the same work item.

findings.title: Visits can never be deleted or relabeled — a botched capture pollutes the timeline and trends forever
findings.detail: CaseStore.delete(patientCase:) (CaseStore.swift:93-98) is never invoked from any view; visit rows in PatientDetailScreen have no swipe actions or context menu, and PatientCase.label is never editable after the save sheet. A mis-capture (wrong patient in frame, bad mesh) permanently distorts the trend strip (PatientDetailScreen.swift:157-223) and sparklines, and a typo in the visit label is uncorrectable.
findings.recommendation: Add swipe-to-delete (with confirmation) and a "Rename visit" context-menu action on visit rows in PatientDetailScreen.

findings.title: Patients/Capture tab split is structurally confusing: Capture is patient-agnostic and the save sheet conflates label with patient code
findings.detail: The Capture tab (ContentView.swift:9-10) produces cases with no patient context, then the save sheet's field is literally titled "Patient code (no PII)" with example "P-014 Visit 2" (AnalysisScreen.swift:430-432) — but the text is stored as a case *label* and the case goes to Unassigned regardless of what code is typed. A practitioner who types "P-014 Visit 2" reasonably expects the case to appear under patient P-014. Two parallel mental models (patient-first via Patients tab vs. capture-first via Capture tab) converge on a save flow that honors neither.
findings.recommendation: Replace the free-text field with a patient picker (existing patients + "New patient" + "Unassigned") and a separate optional visit-label field defaulting to "Visit N"; retitle the field so 'label' and 'patient code' are never conflated.

findings.title: No structured treatment record per visit — the outcome-tracking loop has no 'what was done' half
findings.detail: FaceMap measures the face before and after, but a filler practitioner tracking outcomes needs to record the intervention: product, region(s) injected, units/volume, date of treatment. Today the only vehicle is the free-text visit notes sheet (AnalysisScreen.swift:468-505), and patient-level notes exist in the model (Patient.swift:16) but have no UI at all. Without a treatment record, the Comparison screen shows projection changed +1.2 mm but cannot answer "after what?" — which is the question the practitioner is paying to answer, and a clear moat versus QOVES's measurement-only scoring.
findings.recommendation: Add a lightweight structured "Treatment" entry per visit (region picker from FacialRegion, product free-text, volume in ml) stored on PatientCase and surfaced in ComparisonScreen between visits A and B; also surface Patient.notes in PatientDetailScreen.

findings.title: Stale in-app copy contradicts the current product: v0.2.0 footer, 'four-domain' framework with five domains, 'metrics arrive in v0.3', 'Phase 2' sync
findings.detail: MoreTab.swift:48 shows "v0.2.0" while the app is at v0.5; MoreTab.swift:90 headlines "THE FOUR-DOMAIN FRAMEWORK" then iterates all five FaceDomain cases; AnalysisScreen.swift:254 promises missing-domain metrics "arrive in v0.3" (already shipped era); SettingsScreen.swift:84 says cloud sync is "Coming in Phase 2" while the roadmap says Phase 5. Individually small, together they read as an unmaintained product to a paying clinician evaluating trustworthiness — and the "v0.3" footnote actively misinforms.
findings.recommendation: Sweep copy: read version from Bundle (CFBundleShortVersionString), rename to "five-domain framework", replace the v0.3 footnote with neutral "not yet measured for this capture", align the sync phase wording.

findings.title: Search covers only patient codes — no case search across visit labels or notes
findings.detail: PatientListScreen's search filters on patient.code only (PatientListScreen.swift:71-75). A practitioner who remembers "the chin filler case from March" or text they wrote in notes cannot find it; visit labels and clinician notes are unsearchable, and there is no global case search. With pseudonymous codes as the only identifier, recall depends entirely on memorizing codes — a real workflow gap at >20 patients.
findings.recommendation: Extend the filter to match visit labels and notes (patient matches if any case matches), and show matching-visit subtitles in results.

audits.deviceTestScript:
# FaceMap On-Device Test Script (PM journey verification)

Device: iPhone with TrueDepth (X or newer), app freshly installed.

## 1. First run
1. Launch FaceMap. Confirm the disclaimer gate appears; tap "I am a licensed practitioner — continue".
2. Confirm you land on the Patients tab with the empty state. Note the copy promising an "Unassigned bucket you can re-bind later".

## 2. Patient-first journey (the blocker)
3. Tap "Add patient", create code `P-001`.
4. Open P-001 → tap "Add visit" → complete the 3-pose capture (frontal, oblique L, oblique R).
5. On the Analysis screen tap Save, enter label `Visit 1`, save.
6. EXPECTED BUG: go back to P-001's detail screen — it still says "No visits yet". Return to the Patients list: a patient named "Unassigned" now exists and contains Visit 1.
7. Try to find any way to move Visit 1 from Unassigned to P-001 (long-press the visit row, check Analysis toolbar, check menus). EXPECTED: none exists.

## 3. Stale capture state (cross-visit mixing)
8. Go to the Capture tab. Complete a full 3-pose session and reach Analysis WITHOUT saving; go back.
9. Re-enter the Capture flow and let it auto-capture once. EXPECTED BUG: it captures only Oblique R and immediately jumps to Analysis using the frontal mesh from the PREVIOUS session.

## 4. Comparison journey
10. Under the Unassigned patient (or P-001 if you worked around step 6), save a second visit.
11. In patient detail: tap ellipsis → "Compare visits" → select both visits → "Compare 2/2". Note how many taps this took.
12. In the CHANGES table, find "Surface displacement". EXPECTED BUG: value shown as a tiny percentage (e.g. "0.2%") instead of mm.
13. Look for any share/export button on the Compare screen. EXPECTED: none.

## 5. Settings traps
14. More → Settings → enable "Lock with Face ID" (authenticate). Force-quit and relaunch the app. EXPECTED BUG: app opens straight to patient data with no Face ID prompt.
15. Patients list: swipe-archive a patient. Try to find/restore it anywhere. EXPECTED: impossible.

## 6. Copy check
16. More tab footer should read v0.2.0 (stale); About screen says "FOUR-DOMAIN" above five listed domains; Settings sync says "Phase 2".

audits.summary: FaceMap's screens are visually polished, but the core clinical workflow has two structural UX breaks: a visit captured via a patient's "Add visit" button is silently saved to the "Unassigned" bucket (with no re-bind UI despite the empty state promising one), and the three-pose CaptureScreen never resets after a completed session, so a second capture in the same app run silently reuses stale poses. Error feedback is almost entirely absent — denied camera permission, SwiftData save failures, undecodable cases, and PDF generation failures all degrade silently into misleading or dead UI. Copy is inconsistent with the product's own framework (a "FOUR-DOMAIN" header over five FAS facets, stale v0.2/v0.3 strings, a disclaimer that points to a calibration path that does not exist), which undermines the clinical credibility that differentiates FaceMap from QOVES.

findings.title: "Add visit" from a patient never binds the captured case to that patient; it lands in Unassigned with no re-bind UI
findings.detail: PatientDetailScreen.swift:89-96 pushes CaptureScreen() with no patient parameter (the onDisappear comment 'binding handled in CaptureScreen save' is wrong — CaptureScreen.swift has no patient state). The save sheet in AnalysisScreen.swift:442-459 creates a PatientCase with no patient, and CaseStore.swift:80-83 assigns it to the 'Unassigned' bucket. So a practitioner who taps 'Add visit' on P-014, captures, and saves will find the visit missing from P-014's timeline. Worse, the empty state at PatientListScreen.swift:85 promises 'an Unassigned bucket you can re-bind later', but a repo-wide grep shows no re-bind/move-case UI exists anywhere. Clinical outcome tracking — the product's differentiator — is broken end-to-end for this primary flow.
findings.recommendation: Thread an optional `patient: Patient?` through CaptureScreen → AnalysisScreen → PatientCase init so 'Add visit' saves directly to that patient (pre-fill/skip the label sheet's patient-code semantics). Separately add a 'Move to patient…' action on visit rows (context menu or in AnalysisScreen toolbar) so Unassigned cases can actually be re-bound, or remove the promise from the empty-state copy.

findings.title: CaptureScreen never resets after a completed three-pose session — second capture reuses stale poses
findings.detail: CaptureScreen.swift:19-23 holds `currentPose`, `captures`, and `photos` as @State with no reset in onAppear and no reset after handleSnapshot completes the sequence (CaptureScreen.swift:78-96). After saving and popping back, the screen sits at obliqueR with a full progress bar; the next snapshot re-fires the completion branch (line 87-95) and builds a new MultiPoseCapture mixing the OLD frontal/obliqueL with a NEW obliqueR — silently producing a clinically invalid visit. There is also no 'retake' affordance mid-flow if a pose was captured badly (auto-capture fires after only 0.6s in-window, line 27, so accidental captures are likely).
findings.recommendation: Reset currentPose/captures/photos/poseInWindowSince in .onAppear (or after navigateToAnalysis fires), and add a per-pose 'Retake' control (e.g. tapping a completed segment in the progress bar restarts that pose) plus a 'Start over' button.

findings.title: Denied camera permission leaves the user stuck on 'Position your face in front of the camera' forever
findings.detail: FaceCaptureView.swift never checks AVCaptureDevice.authorizationStatus and the Coordinator implements no session(_:didFailWithError:) (FaceCaptureView.swift:41-62, 111-128). When camera access is denied, ARSession fails, no frames arrive, trackingState stays .noFace, and CaptureScreen.swift:172 shows 'Position your face in front of the camera' indefinitely — actively misleading feedback with no path to Settings. Same dead-end for any other AR session failure (sensor in use, thermal shutdown).
findings.recommendation: Add an ARSessionDelegate didFailWithError handler and a pre-flight AVCaptureDevice.authorizationStatus(for: .video) check; introduce a .permissionDenied / .sessionFailed TrackingState that CaptureScreen renders as an explanatory card with a 'Open Settings' button (UIApplication.openSettingsURLString).

findings.title: Save failures are invisible: the save sheet always dismisses as if the case was persisted
findings.detail: AnalysisScreen.swift:456-458 calls store.save(pc) then unconditionally dismisses both the sheet and the screen. CaseStore.swift:85-91 catches the SwiftData error and only logs it — the practitioner sees a successful-looking dismissal while the visit silently vanished. Notes and annotation edits use bare `try? store.context.save()` (AnalysisScreen.swift:142, 170) with the same silent-loss behavior. For a clinical record-keeping app, unacknowledged data loss is the worst possible failure mode (this deepens the known '33 silent try? sites' gap with the specific user-facing consequence).
findings.recommendation: Make CaseStore.save (and a saveContext helper) return Result/throw; on failure keep the sheet open and show an alert ('Could not save this visit — try again'). Apply the same pattern to notes/annotation persistence with a lightweight toast or retry alert.

findings.title: First-run experience never explains FAS/AART-HIT, the wheel, or the metric jargon at point of use
findings.detail: After the disclaimer gate, the new user lands on an empty patient list and then a camera with yaw-degree prompts; nothing introduces the five-facet Facial Assessment Scale that organizes the entire product. 'FAS' appears only in code comments (FaceDomain.swift:3-9, FASRow.swift:3) — never in any UI string. The first AnalysisScreen the user sees is dense with unexplained notation: 'worst |Δ from φ| %', 'worst Z-deficit mm', 'min °(target …)' (AnalysisScreen.swift:536-560), and the AestheticWheel has no legend or tap-to-explain. The only framework explanation is buried under More → 'About Dr Andreas Nikolis' (MoreTab.swift:90-123). Compared with QOVES's consumer-simple output, FaceMap's clinical depth reads as noise on first contact.
findings.recommendation: Add a 2-3 card first-run intro after the disclaimer (what FAS is, what the wheel shows, the 3-pose capture + calibrate-once flow), name the scale in the Analysis header or wheel ('FAS facets'), and give each metric row an info affordance reusing the existing per-metric blurbs from AboutScreen.domainBlurb.

findings.title: Disclaimer instructs a calibration path that does not exist, and a new user cannot calibrate at all before their first capture
findings.detail: DisclaimerCopy.swift:15-17 says calibrate via 'More → Calibrate landmarks', but the More tab item (MoreTab.swift:28-33, labeled 'Landmark calibration') opens only a read-only explainer; actual calibration lives behind the unlabeled scope icon in the Analysis toolbar (AnalysisScreen.swift:389-397) and requires an already-captured face (CalibrationScreen.swift:8 takes `face: CapturedFace`). So the very first thing the app tells the user to do is impossible until after they have captured a case whose results the warning banner simultaneously declares 'not clinically meaningful' (DisclaimerCopy.swift:27-29). The explainer's step list (MoreTab.swift:142-148) is the only place the scope icon is ever named.
findings.recommendation: Fix the disclaimer copy to describe the real path; add a 'Calibrate now' button on the CalibrationExplainerScreen that launches a throwaway capture for calibration purposes; consider a labeled 'Calibrate' row in the uncalibrated warning banner flow rather than relying on the toolbar scope glyph.

findings.title: Compare — a flagship differentiator — is buried three interactions deep behind an ellipsis menu
findings.detail: Visit-over-visit comparison is reachable only via PatientDetailScreen's toolbar ellipsis → 'Compare visits' → multi-select mode → a small 'Compare 2/2' text button that is disabled until exactly two rows are checked (PatientDetailScreen.swift:43-62, 229-239). Nothing on the screen advertises that comparison exists; a practitioner with 2+ visits gets no prompt. The fallback navigationDestination branch 'Pick exactly two visits to compare.' (PatientDetailScreen.swift:69) is a dead-end debug string that should be unreachable but ships as UI copy.
findings.recommendation: Surface a prominent 'Compare latest two visits' button on PatientDetailScreen whenever sortedCases.count >= 2 (pre-selecting the two most recent), keep multi-select for arbitrary pairs, and remove the unreachable fallback text.

findings.title: Terminology contradiction: 'THE FOUR-DOMAIN FRAMEWORK' header lists five facets, and the bio copy names four completely different domains
findings.detail: AboutScreen renders 'THE FOUR-DOMAIN FRAMEWORK' (MoreTab.swift:90) directly above a ForEach over FaceDomain.allCases, which has five cases (skinQuality, facialShape, proportions, symmetry, expression — MoreTab.swift:115-123, FaceDomain.swift). The DisclaimerCopy.aboutNikolis text shown immediately above it (DisclaimerCopy.swift:36-46) describes a different taxonomy entirely — 'Mechanical behaviour, Optical properties, Symmetry & proportions, Structural volume … four-domain analysis' — which matches nothing in the UI. For an app whose credibility rests on the published FAS framework, the About screen contradicting itself within one scroll is damaging. A vestige of the pre-migration enum (see commit 5279c57).
findings.recommendation: Update the header to 'THE FIVE FAS FACETS' (or similar) and rewrite aboutNikolis to describe the actual five-facet Facial Assessment Scale; flag the bio for Dr Nikolis review since the file comment marks it placeholder.

findings.title: Save sheet conflates patient code with visit label, guaranteeing user confusion about where data goes
findings.detail: The AnalysisScreen save sheet's TextField placeholder is 'Patient code (no PII)' but the footer example is 'P-014 Visit 2' and the value is stored as the case *label*, not a patient binding (AnalysisScreen.swift:430-433, 447-455). A user typing 'P-014 Visit 2' will reasonably expect the visit to appear under patient P-014; instead it appears under 'Unassigned' with that string as its name. Even after the patient-binding fix (finding 1), ad-hoc captures from the Capture tab will keep this sheet, so the field must be honest about being a visit label.
findings.recommendation: Rename the field to 'Visit label' with footer 'e.g. "Visit 2 — pre-treatment"', and add an explicit patient picker (defaulting to Unassigned) to the save sheet so the destination is visible at save time.

findings.title: Undecodable saved cases render as a dimmed, untappable row with zero explanation
findings.detail: PatientCase.capturedFace returns nil on any JSON decode failure (PatientCase.swift:88). PatientDetailScreen.swift:268-270 then renders the visit row at opacity 0.5 with no NavigationLink and no message — the practitioner sees a ghost row that does nothing when tapped, with no indication the underlying mesh data is unreadable or what to do about it. ComparisonScreen handles the same condition better with placeholderMesh (ComparisonScreen.swift:113-114), but its region-change .task silently skips analysis when either face is nil (ComparisonScreen.swift:38-43), leaving the region-change section to communicate absence implicitly.
findings.recommendation: Replace the opacity-0.5 treatment with an explicit 'Mesh data unavailable' badge on the row and allow opening a degraded detail view (metrics/notes still decode independently); in ComparisonScreen show an inline note when region-change analysis was skipped because a mesh failed to load.

findings.title: PDF export fails silently — tapping the share icon can simply do nothing
findings.detail: AnalysisScreen.exportPDF (AnalysisScreen.swift:510-519) has two silent guard-return paths: missing patient and TreatmentPlanPDF.generate returning nil. The user taps the export toolbar button and nothing happens — no spinner, no error. Given TreatmentPlanPDF is known-stale (predates photos/region-changes/new metrics), generate-failure is a live risk, and PDF rendering of a 600x400 mesh snapshot plus generation runs synchronously on the main actor with no progress indication even on success.
findings.recommendation: Show an alert on either failure path ('Could not generate the PDF for this visit'), and add a brief progress overlay while generating; log the underlying reason for support.

findings.title: AnalysisScreen has no loading state while metrics and skin-quality analysis compute; pose switching shows stale results
findings.detail: evaluateMetrics is async and includes the photo-based SkinQualityAnalyzer (AnalysisScreen.swift:415-422). On first appearance `results` is empty so the entire 'FINDINGS BY DOMAIN' section is simply absent (findingsSection guard at AnalysisScreen.swift:203) — the screen looks finished-but-empty, then content pops in. On pose change (AnalysisScreen.swift:150-154) the previous pose's results remain on screen until the recompute lands, so for a beat the findings list mismatches the displayed mesh — a subtle but real clinical-trust problem.
findings.recommendation: Add an isEvaluating state: show a 'Analyzing…' placeholder card on first load and dim/overlay the findings list (or clear results) during pose-switch recomputes so stale numbers are never presented against the wrong mesh.

findings.title: Stale version and roadmap strings ship in user-facing copy: 'v0.2.0', 'metrics arrive in v0.3', 'Coming soon'
findings.detail: The More tab footer hard-codes 'FaceMap by Dr Andreas Nikolis · v0.2.0' (MoreTab.swift:48) while the app is at v0.5. AnalysisScreen's unpopulated-domain footnote says '<domains> metrics arrive in v0.3.' (AnalysisScreen.swift:254) — a promise about a release that already shipped, shown beneath every analysis. PatientDetailScreen trend cards show 'Coming soon' for empty domains (PatientDetailScreen.swift:180) even when the real reason is the patient's visits predate those metrics. Stale roadmap copy in a clinical tool erodes confidence in the measurements themselves.
findings.recommendation: Read the version from Bundle (CFBundleShortVersionString) instead of hard-coding; change the footnote to a factual 'No <domain> metrics for this capture' and the trend card to 'No data yet'.

findings.title: Patient creation allows duplicate and 'Untitled' codes with no validation feedback
findings.detail: CaseStore.createPatient silently substitutes 'Untitled' for whitespace-only codes (CaseStore.swift:54-59) and nothing checks for duplicate codes, so two 'P-014' patients can coexist, and the rename sheet (PatientDetailScreen.swift:327-353) has the same gap. Because patients are identified solely by pseudonymous code (no PII by design), duplicate codes are indistinguishable in the list — a real mis-filing risk in a clinic. Search has no 'no results' empty state either: filtering to zero matches shows a bare list with just the section header (PatientListScreen.swift:24-47).
findings.recommendation: On create/rename, check for an existing active patient with the same trimmed code and show inline 'This code is already in use'; add a simple 'No patients match "<query>"' empty view for the search case.

findings.title: Archive via swipe is instant and unexplained, with no undo and no way to view archived patients
findings.detail: PatientListScreen.swift:32-34 archives on swipe-delete with no confirmation and no undo toast; the iOS swipe action will display the default 'Delete' label/red styling even though the action is an archive, and the footer hint 'Swipe a row to archive' (PatientListScreen.swift:38) is the only disclosure. CaseStore has unarchive() (CaseStore.swift:67-70) but no screen ever lists archived patients, so an accidental swipe makes a patient and their entire visit history unreachable forever from the UI.
findings.recommendation: Use a custom .swipeActions labeled 'Archive' (non-destructive tint), show an 'Archived — Undo' toast, and add an 'Archived patients' section/screen (e.g. under More) that exposes unarchive.

audits.deviceTestScript:
# FaceMap On-Device UX Test Script (TrueDepth iPhone required)

## A. First run
1. Delete the app if installed; install a fresh build and launch.
2. Confirm the disclaimer screen appears; read the calibration paragraph, then tap "I am a licensed practitioner — continue".
3. **Check:** You land on an empty Patients list. Note whether anything explains FAS, the wheel, or the 3-pose capture (expected finding: nothing does).
4. Follow the disclaimer's instruction literally: go to More → "Landmark calibration". **Check:** it is only an explainer — you cannot actually calibrate from here.

## B. Camera permission failure
5. Go to the Capture tab; when iOS asks for camera access, tap **Don't Allow**.
6. **Check (bug):** the screen says "Position your face in front of the camera" forever, with no error or Settings link.
7. Re-enable camera in iOS Settings → FaceMap before continuing.

## C. Patient + Add visit binding (blocker)
8. Patients tab → + → create patient `P-001`.
9. Open P-001 → tap **Add visit** → complete the coached frontal/oblique-L/oblique-R capture → on the Analysis screen tap **Save**, enter `P-001 Visit 1`, save.
10. **Check (bug):** return to P-001's detail screen — the visit is NOT in the timeline. Go back to Patients: an "Unassigned" patient now holds the visit.
11. Try to move that visit to P-001. **Check (bug):** there is no way to do it.

## D. Capture state reuse (blocker)
12. Without relaunching the app, go to the Capture tab again.
13. **Check (bug):** the progress bar already shows completed poses. Capture one pose; you are taken straight to a "new" analysis built from the previous session's frontal capture mixed with the new pose.
14. Relaunch the app to clear the state.

## E. Analysis screen feedback
15. Open a saved visit. **Check:** note the gap before "FINDINGS BY DOMAIN" appears (no loading indicator), and whether the metric strings (|Δ from φ|, Z-deficit) are explained anywhere.
16. Switch poses with the F/L/R pills quickly. **Check:** whether the findings list briefly shows the previous pose's numbers.
17. Tap the share (export) icon on a visit. If a PDF appears, check it for the photos and new metrics (expected stale). If nothing happens at all, that is the silent-failure bug.

## F. Compare discoverability
18. Capture and save a second visit for any patient (via Unassigned if needed).
19. On the patient detail screen, find how to compare two visits without hints. **Check:** it requires ellipsis menu → "Compare visits" → check two rows → small "Compare 2/2" button.

## G. Copy checks
20. More tab footer: **Check** version string says v0.2.0 (stale).
21. More → About Dr Andreas Nikolis: **Check** "THE FOUR-DOMAIN FRAMEWORK" header sits above a list of FIVE facets, and the bio paragraph names four different domains (Mechanical/Optical/Symmetry/Structural).
22. On any analysis with missing domains, **check** for the "...metrics arrive in v0.3." footnote.

## H. Archive safety
23. On the Patients list, swipe a patient row. **Check:** the action label/color (likely red "Delete" for what is actually an archive), no confirmation, no undo, and no screen anywhere to find archived patients afterward.

audits.summary: The design system core (Theme.swift, Typography.swift) is well-conceived — light chrome, domain-hue severity ramps, serif/mono pairing — but adoption is leaky: three whole screens use zero Theme tokens, components invent micro font sizes the scale lacks, and domain hues are repeatedly repurposed as status colors, eroding the wheel's color semantics. The biggest brand risks are (a) iOS and web assign the same hex values to different FAS facets, so the identity contradicts itself across platforms, and (b) the aesthetic wheel paints two facets (Skin Quality + Expression) into the same quadrant with overlapping labels and an unreachable tap target. The PDF is the weakest deliverable for a practitioner-facing product: it predates clinical photos, region-projection tracking, and the new metrics, mis-formats non-ratio metric units, and uses an ad-hoc 14-size type ramp with 8pt body text.

findings.title: iOS and web disagree on which color means which FAS facet
findings.detail: Web facetHues (web/lib/tokens.ts:36-41) define skinQuality=#C9BBEE (lavender), proportions=#7A8094 (slate), expression=#F2C9A1 (peach). iOS Theme.swift:31-36 defines skinQuality=0x7A8094 (slate), expression=0xC9BBEE (lavender), proportions=0x9AB2D6 (a soft blue that exists nowhere on the web), and has no peach at all. So lavender means 'skin quality' on the marketing site but 'expression' in the app, and slate flips between 'proportions' and 'skin quality'. LogoMark.swift:25-30 and web/components/brand-mark.tsx:13-16 consequently render differently-ordered quadrants. For a brand whose entire identity is the facet wheel, the same hue meaning two different clinical facets across touchpoints is a real credibility problem — a practitioner who reads the site then opens the app gets contradictory color legends, and the PDF (which patients may compare against facemap.app) inherits the iOS mapping.
findings.recommendation: Declare web facetHues the source of truth (it has a complete 5-hue set including peach #F2C9A1). Update Theme.swift: domainSkinQuality=0xC9BBEE, domainFacialShape=0xA6B4DD (unchanged), domainProportions=0x7A8094 (+ keep 0x3F4456 as its fill), domainSymmetry=0xE9B5E0 (unchanged), domainExpression=0xF2C9A1. Update LogoMark.swift slice order to match brand-mark.tsx, and add a comment in both files pointing at the other as the mirror. Single PR, mechanical change.

findings.title: Aesthetic wheel double-books the top-left quadrant: Skin Quality and Expression overlap
findings.detail: AestheticWheel.swift:196-207 maps both .skinQuality and .expression to the (180,270) quadrant. Consequences: (1) both quadrant gradient fills are painted into the same sector (drawWheel loop at :53-67), so whichever facet comes later in FaceDomain.allCases overpaints the other; (2) labelOverlay (:156-171) positions 'SKIN QUALITY' and 'EXPRESSION' at the identical midAngle point, rendering them on top of each other as unreadable text — this also appears in the patient-facing PDF (TreatmentPlanPDF.swift:144 renders the wheel with showsLabels: true); (3) domain(forAngle:) at :219-228 always resolves the quadrant tap to .skinQuality, so the Expression domain detail is unreachable from the wheel. The code comment at :197-199 acknowledges a 5-sector layout is a v0.6 follow-up — this deepens it: it is not just a layout nicety, it is overlapping text in the exported clinical document and a dead navigation path.
findings.recommendation: Implement the 5-sector wheel now: 72° sectors, anchored so Skin Quality starts at 12 o'clock and order matches the web FAS presentation; update quadrantBoundsDegrees, domain(forAngle:), and label placement (labels will need slight radial offset or curved baseline at 72° spacing). Keep the 3-ring severity geometry untouched. Update LogoMark to a 5-slice mark in the same pass (see icon brief) so wheel, logo, and web stay congruent.

findings.title: No status colors in Theme, so screens steal domain hues for success/warning/error — corrupting the color language
findings.detail: Theme.swift deliberately avoids a red/amber/green ramp (comment at Theme.swift:5-6), but provides no semantic status tokens at all. Result: (1) CaptureScreen.swift:133 and :163 use Theme.domainSymmetry (magenta-pink) for both the 'current pose' chip and the 'unsupported device' badge — pink simultaneously means 'Symmetry facet', 'you are here', and 'hardware error'; (2) ComparisonScreen.swift:202 uses Theme.domainSymmetry for 'projection lost'; (3) CalibrationWarningBanner.swift:11-29 hardcodes four amber hexes (0xB45309, 0x78350F, 0xFEF3C7) that exist nowhere in Theme; (4) TreatmentPlanPDF.swift:235-238 hardcodes a different amber (Color(red:0.47...) / Color(red:1.0,0.95,0.78)) for the same calibration warning, so app banner and PDF strip don't even match each other; (5) CalibrationScreen.swift:150 and RegionCalibrationScreen.swift:156 fall back to system .green for 'picked'. In a clinical tool where the wheel's pastel hues carry diagnostic meaning, reusing them for status destroys that meaning.
findings.recommendation: Add to Theme: warning (0xB45309), warningBg (0xFEF3C7), warningInk (0x78350F), positive (a desaturated green, e.g. 0x3E7C4F), negative (a desaturated brick, e.g. 0x9B3B2E) — chosen to sit outside the five pastel facet hues. Migrate the five call sites above plus the PDF warning strip to these tokens. Forbid domain hues for non-domain meaning via a code comment in Theme.swift.

findings.title: Three screens bypass the design system entirely (zero Theme/Type tokens)
findings.detail: CalibrationScreen.swift, RegionCalibrationScreen.swift, and MeshFullScreen.swift contain no reference to Theme or Type at all (verified by grep across FaceMap/UI). They use system .headline/.callout/.caption fonts (CalibrationScreen.swift:142-150, RegionCalibrationScreen.swift:148-174, MeshFullScreen.swift:33,66,78), system .secondary foreground, system .bordered/.borderedProminent buttons (CalibrationScreen.swift:154-171) which render iOS-default blue, raw Color.cyan as a toggle-active color (CalibrationScreen.swift:121), and .regularMaterial cards. These are practitioner-facing setup screens, not debug UI — they currently look like a different app (default blue buttons and cyan accents against FaceMap's near-black-ink/pastel identity). The serif metric voice and PrimaryButton/SecondaryButton styles (Buttons.swift) are simply absent here.
findings.recommendation: Migrate the three screens to Type.* fonts, Theme ink/surface tokens, and the existing PrimaryButton/SecondaryButton styles; replace Color.cyan with Theme.ink-on-material or the new warning/positive tokens; replace .green 'picked' confirmation with the new positive token. Mesh-viewport overlays may keep white-on-black per the meshCanvas spotlight rule, but should still use Type sizes.

findings.title: Type scale lacks micro sizes, so even DesignSystem components hardcode fonts
findings.detail: Typography.swift bottoms out at 11pt (sectionHeader) and 12pt (caption). But the UI needs 9-10pt labels constantly, so components invent them inline: AestheticWheel.swift:167,178 (9pt), DomainBadge.swift:15 (10pt), SummaryHeader.swift:46 (10pt), BrandMark.swift:54 (9pt), CaptureScreen.swift:137 (9pt), MetricRow.swift:35 and CategoryRow.swift:60 (12pt semibold duplicating captionStrong), Buttons.swift:6,22 (16pt medium — a button size missing from the scale), MeshFullScreen.swift:33 (14pt semibold). Roughly 50 .font(.system(size:...)) call sites exist outside Typography.swift. None of these can participate in Dynamic Type either, since the whole scale uses fixed sizes rather than relativeTo: metrics — a secondary but real accessibility concern for 40-60-year-old practitioners.
findings.recommendation: Add to Type: micro (9pt semibold, tracked — wheel/diagram labels), labelSmall (10pt semibold — badges), button (16pt medium), control (14pt semibold — overlay chips). Sweep the ~50 call sites to tokens (mechanical). As a follow-up, rebuild the scale with Font.system(size:relativeTo:) for Dynamic Type support.

findings.title: PDF report is stale and structurally wrong for the v0.5 feature set; v2 layout spec
findings.detail: TreatmentPlanPDF.swift renders one fixed A4 page (TreatmentPlanPDF.swift:30-38 only ever begins one PDF page). Problems as a patient-handout: (1) clinical photos exist (PatientCase.photos, saved at AnalysisScreen.swift:454) but the report shows only a black mesh snapshot (:128-141) — a wireframe face is alienating to patients; the frontal photo is the single most persuasive artifact a practitioner can hand over; (2) region-projection changes (SurfaceChangeAnalyzer / ComparisonScreen) — the product's core differentiator vs QOVES — are absent; (3) metric list is unbounded (:155-159) and the layout relies on a Spacer (:81) before the signature block, so 7 metrics + pins + notes will silently compress or collide rather than paginate; (4) formatPDFValue (:287-297) special-cases only canthal tilt and asymmetry, so the new surface-displacement and expression-asymmetry metrics fall into the default '%.1f%% (≤ %.0f%%)' branch and print wrong units in a signed clinical document; (5) the wheel is rendered with showsLabels:true at 200pt inside a 220pt slot — labelOverlay places text at radius*1.02 (AestheticWheel.swift:158), outside the declared frame, so labels clip in the rendered PDF; (6) no photo/identity area means the document fails the basic 'is this me' test for the patient.
findings.recommendation: v2 layout, two pages, loop context.beginPDFPage per page: PAGE 1 (patient-facing): header (brandmark, practitioner, patient code, visit date); 3-up clinical-photo triptych (oblique L / frontal / oblique R, ~160pt tall, 6pt radius, captions); aesthetic wheel (5-sector, labels with proper bleed margin) beside a 'Key findings' box of the top 3 non-normal metrics in plain language; signature block. PAGE 2 (clinical detail): metric table grouped by FAS facet with facet hue tab on each group, per-metric unit-correct formatting (move formatting onto the metric type, not a switch in the PDF); 'Change since last visit' section listing region projection deltas with the ±0.3mm noise-floor note when a prior visit exists; annotation pins; notes; calibration strip and footer disclaimer on both pages. Add a metric-count overflow rule (continue table to page 3) instead of Spacer-crush.

findings.title: PDF has no type scale — 14 ad-hoc font sizes, body text at 8-9pt
findings.detail: TreatmentPlanPDF.swift hardcodes .system(size:) at 28, 12, 10, 9, 8pt across ~15 call sites (e.g. :101, :105, :111-118, :191-201, :230-232, :249, :267-271, :282) with no PDFType companion to PDFTheme. Footer disclaimer and the calibration warning render at 8pt — at A4/72dpi that is below comfortable print legibility, and the legally-significant disclaimer is the smallest text on the page. Metric values (the clinical payload) are 9pt while the wordmark is 28pt. PDFTheme.swift covers only colors and page geometry (PDFTheme.swift:5-18). The app's serif-for-metric-names convention (Type.metricName) is also dropped in the PDF, so the handout doesn't carry the product's typographic voice.
findings.recommendation: Add a PDFType enum to PDFTheme.swift: display 24 serif tracked, sectionHeader 9 semibold tracked, body 10, metricName 10.5 serif, value 10 mono, caption 9, legal 8.5 minimum. Migrate all .system(size:) calls in TreatmentPlanPDF.swift; bump disclaimer to at least 8.5-9pt and metric values to 10pt mono.

findings.title: App icon: design brief + Assets.xcassets build spec (currently nonexistent, pbxproj references it)
findings.detail: No .xcassets exists anywhere in the repo (verified by find), yet both build configs set ASSETCATALOG_COMPILER_APPICON_NAME = AppIcon (FaceMap.xcodeproj/project.pbxproj:729 and :746), so the app ships with a blank icon and App Store submission would fail. BRIEF — Concept: the FAS wheel is already the brand (LogoMark.swift, brand-mark.tsx); the icon should be the wheel mark, no wordmark, no face imagery (a face photo/mesh icon reads consumer-cosmetic, QOVES territory; an abstract instrument reads clinical). Geometry: reuse brand-mark.tsx proportions — full-bleed circle of radius 15/16 of half-width, hub at 4/15 of outer radius; after the 5-facet migration, five 72° sectors starting at 12 o'clock, separated by 2.5%-width canvas-colored slits (mirroring AestheticWheel's quadrant gaps at AestheticWheel.swift:81-95); thin ink ring (0x0A0A0F at 3% width) around the circle. Colors: the five facet hues from the unified palette (0xC9BBEE, 0xA6B4DD, 0x7A8094, 0xE9B5E0, 0xF2C9A1) on canvas 0xFAFAFA background; hub filled canvas with hairline ink ring. Dark variant (iOS 18): same wheel on 0x0A0A0F with hub 0x0A0A0F; tinted variant: sectors as opacity steps 0.38/0.52/0.64/0.82/1.0 of white (reusing the severity ramp as a brand gesture). The pastels need the slit + ink ring to hold up at 60px; verify at 29pt settings size.
findings.recommendation: BUILD SPEC: (1) create FaceMap/Assets.xcassets/Contents.json ({"info":{"author":"xcode","version":1}}); (2) AppIcon.appiconset/Contents.json with the modern single-size form: images: [{idiom:universal, platform:ios, size:1024x1024, filename:AppIcon.png}, plus {appearances:[{appearance:luminosity,value:dark}]} and tinted entries]; (3) generate the three 1024x1024 PNGs by scripting the SVG geometry above (a small Swift CLI using CoreGraphics, or rsvg-convert from an .svg checked into web/public) — the geometry is fully specified by brand-mark.tsx so this is automatable; (4) add an AccentColor.colorset (0x0A0A0F) and set ASSETCATALOG_COMPILER_GLOBAL_ACCENT_COLOR_NAME; (5) add the xcassets folder reference to the FaceMap target in project.pbxproj. No human input required except final visual sign-off.

findings.title: Comparison deltas: salience of improvement vs worsening is inverted, and the encoding is opacity-blind
findings.detail: ComparisonScreen.swift:286-295: an improved metric gets a near-black ink arrow (maximum visual weight) while a worsened metric gets the pastel domain hue (decorative weight) — the practitioner's eye is pulled to good news and skates over regressions, the opposite of clinical priority. The arrow metaphor is also ambiguous: 'arrow.down.right' means improved (severity went down) but down-right arrows conventionally read negative. In regionChangeRow (:198-218), 'projection gained' = ink and 'projection lost' = pink, with the same inversion problem; for filler patients, gained projection in a treated region is usually the desired outcome, so neither pole should borrow a facet hue. Severity legibility generally: the mild step of the ramp is domain hue at 0.38 opacity over near-white (Theme.swift:82-89) — for E9B5E0 and C9BBEE that is roughly a 1.2-1.4:1 contrast against the canvas in an 8-10pt dot (SeverityDot.swift:10-16), effectively invisible in peripheral scanning and unprintable; severity is encoded by opacity alone in list rows (no shape/text redundancy), which fails for color-vision deficiency.
findings.recommendation: Use the new positive/negative status tokens for delta arrows (worsened = negative brick, improved = positive green-gray, unchanged = inkMuted) and swap glyphs to checkmark/exclamation-style or up/down relative to severity with a text label ('Improved'/'Worsened') which severityChangeLabel already provides — give it the status color too. For SeverityDot, add redundancy: keep hue+opacity but render ringIndex as 0-3 filled segments or pair the dot with the existing severity text everywhere it appears standalone.

findings.title: Theme has no spacing scale; 13 distinct padding values in use
findings.detail: Theme.swift defines radii and hairline width but no spacing tokens. The UI layer uses raw paddings 4, 6, 8, 10, 12, 13, 14, 16, 24, 28, 32, 40, 48 (frequency survey across FaceMap/UI: 16 appears 38x, 8 23x, 12 13x, plus oddballs like 13 at Buttons.swift:25 and 14, 28 once each). Card interiors drift between 6, 8, and 12 across MetricRow/ComparisonScreen/PDF rows, which is visible as uneven rhythm when cards stack on the analysis and comparison screens.
findings.recommendation: Add Theme.space: s4=4, s8=8, s12=12, s16=16, s24=24, s32=32, s40=40; normalize 6→8 (or keep 6 as s6 for dense PDF rows), 10→8 or 12, 13/14→12 or 16. Mechanical sweep, good lint-rule candidate afterwards.

findings.title: LogoMark doc comment and quadrant mapping are stale relative to the 5-facet enum
findings.detail: LogoMark.swift:3-10 still documents quadrants as 'Theme.domainOptical / domainStructural / domainMechanical' — token names that no longer exist (renamed in Theme.swift:31-36) — and the mark renders only 4 of the 5 facets, omitting Expression entirely, while the web brand-mark also draws 4 quadrants using the old domainHues object (brand-mark.tsx:13-16) rather than facetHues. The brand mark therefore tells a 4-domain story while the product, the wheel, and the marketing copy tell a 5-facet (FAS) story. This matters for the icon brief above: whichever sector count is chosen must be applied to LogoMark, brand-mark.tsx, and the app icon together.
findings.recommendation: Decide once: 5-slice mark (recommended — matches FAS and the v0.6 wheel) and update LogoMark.swift slices/comments, brand-mark.tsx paths (five 72° arcs), and derive the app icon from it. If the 4-quadrant mark is kept deliberately as a simplified logo, document that decision in both files so the next agent doesn't 'fix' it.

audits.summary: The metric/analyzer core is well-tested and defensively coded (range-guarded indexing, topology-mismatch guard, NaN-tolerant formatting), but the persistence and capture seams are fragile: corrupt stored-mesh JSON can hard-crash the app, capture has zero handling for session failure/interruption or camera-permission denial, and every failure path (save, photo encode, PDF export) is silent. Most critically, a visit saved via "Add visit" from a patient's screen is never bound to that patient — it lands in the "Unassigned" bucket with no rebind UI, which breaks the core visit-over-visit comparison workflow FaceMap differentiates on. Test coverage is good for metric math but absent for Codable round-trips, corrupt payloads, calibration-store hostile inputs, and PDF generation.

findings.title: Saved visits never bind to the patient they were captured for — all new cases land in 'Unassigned'
findings.detail: PatientDetailScreen's 'Add visit' pushes CaptureScreen() with no patient context (/Users/fuannegao/Documents/PERSONAL_WEBSITE/FACEMAP/FaceMap/UI/PatientDetailScreen.swift:89-92). The save sheet in AnalysisScreen constructs PatientCase without a patient (/Users/fuannegao/Documents/PERSONAL_WEBSITE/FACEMAP/FaceMap/UI/AnalysisScreen.swift:447-456), and CaseStore.save rebinds nil-patient cases to the 'Unassigned' bucket (/Users/fuannegao/Documents/PERSONAL_WEBSITE/FACEMAP/FaceMap/Cases/CaseStore.swift:80-84). PatientListScreen.swift:85 promises an 'Unassigned bucket you can re-bind later' but no rebind UI exists anywhere (grep confirms only bootstrap migration sets .patient). Net effect: the Visit-2-vs-Visit-1 ComparisonScreen flow — the product's key differentiator — only works by comparing inside the Unassigned bucket; per-patient timelines, trends, and PDF patient codes are all broken for new captures.
findings.recommendation: Thread an optional `patient: Patient?` through CaptureScreen → AnalysisScreen → the save sheet, pass it to PatientCase(init patient:), and pre-fill the label with the patient code. Separately add a 'Move to patient…' action on visit rows so Unassigned cases can be rebound. Add a PatientMigrationTests case asserting a save initiated with a patient binds to it.

findings.title: Corrupt CapturedFace JSON crashes the app instead of degrading to 'Mesh unreadable'
findings.detail: CapturedFace uses synthesized Codable, so any [Float]/[Int16] payload decodes. But: (1) the `vertices` accessor indexes vertexData[i+1]/[i+2] in a stride-3 loop with no multiple-of-3 check — a truncated array traps (/Users/fuannegao/Documents/PERSONAL_WEBSITE/FACEMAP/FaceMap/Capture/CapturedFace.swift:39-48); (2) the `transform` accessor indexes d[0]…d[15] with no count check (CapturedFace.swift:50-58); (3) FaceMeshOverlay maps triangle indices with `UInt32($0)` — any negative Int16 traps at runtime (/Users/fuannegao/Documents/PERSONAL_WEBSITE/FACEMAP/FaceMap/Visualization/FaceMeshOverlay.swift:219 `d.primitives = .triangles(face.triangleIndices.map { UInt32($0) })`). PatientCase's `try?` decode (PatientCase.swift:87-99) only protects against malformed JSON, not structurally-valid-but-corrupt values, so one bad externally-stored blob makes a patient record permanently crash the visit list/compare/analysis screens. Currently the only handled corrupt case is full decode failure → nil → ComparisonScreen 'Mesh unreadable' placeholder (ComparisonScreen.swift:123-130) and dimmed unopenable rows (PatientDetailScreen.swift:268-270).
findings.recommendation: Give CapturedFace a custom init(from:) that validates vertexData.count % 3 == 0, transformData.count == 16, triangleIndices.count % 3 == 0, all indices in 0..<vertexCount, and throws otherwise (decode failure then flows into the existing nil/'Mesh unreadable' paths). Add unit tests feeding truncated vertexData, 15-float transforms, and negative triangle indices through JSONDecoder.

findings.title: No ARSession failure/interruption handling: camera denial hangs silently; backgrounding mid-hold can fire a stale, wrong-pose snapshot
findings.detail: FaceCaptureView.Coordinator implements only session(_:didUpdate:) (/Users/fuannegao/Documents/PERSONAL_WEBSITE/FACEMAP/FaceMap/Capture/FaceCaptureView.swift:41-62). There is no session(_:didFailWithError:), sessionWasInterrupted, or sessionInterruptionEnded. Consequences: (a) if the user denies camera permission, the session fails and the UI sits forever on 'Position your face in front of the camera' (CaptureScreen.swift:171-172 only distinguishes unsupported/noFace/tracking) with no path to Settings; (b) if the app backgrounds during the 0.6s hold after triggerCapture set pendingSnapshot=true (FaceCaptureView.swift:24-25, CaptureScreen.swift:119-123), the 10-frame buffer retains pre-interruption geometry, and on resume the snapshot fires on the first frame regardless of current head pose — averaging stale frames with new ones and recording a potentially wrong-pose capture into the clinical record with no warning; isCapturing also stays true (overlay stuck) until that delayed snapshot lands.
findings.recommendation: Implement the three missing ARSessionDelegate methods: on interruption/failure clear recentGeometries and pendingSnapshot and emit a new TrackingState (.interrupted / .permissionDenied with a Settings deep-link button). In CaptureScreen, also reset poseInWindowSince/isCapturing on scenePhase != .active. Additionally re-verify the pose window at snapshot time (drop the snapshot if yaw left the window).

findings.title: Case save failures are completely silent — a visit can be 'saved' as empty Data and the clinician told nothing
findings.detail: PatientCase.init swallows encode failures with `(try? JSONEncoder().encode(...)) ?? Data()` (/Users/fuannegao/Documents/PERSONAL_WEBSITE/FACEMAP/FaceMap/Cases/PatientCase.swift:56-57,62-63), so a case can persist with an empty mesh blob; on reload capturedFace decodes to nil and the visit row is permanently dimmed/unopenable. CaseStore.save catches context.save() errors and only logs (/Users/fuannegao/Documents/PERSONAL_WEBSITE/FACEMAP/FaceMap/Cases/CaseStore.swift:85-91), while AnalysisScreen's Save button unconditionally dismisses as if it succeeded (AnalysisScreen.swift:456-458). For a clinical record-keeping app, data loss must surface to the user. This is the sharpest instance of the known 33-silent-try? gap because it loses the primary artifact of a patient visit.
findings.recommendation: Make CaseStore.save throwing (or return Result), make PatientCase.init throwing for the mesh encode (metric results/annotations can stay best-effort), and show an alert from the save sheet on failure instead of dismissing. Add a test that a PatientCase whose capturedFaceJSON is empty Data round-trips to capturedFace == nil (documenting the legacy fallback).

findings.title: Calibration stores accept out-of-range/negative indices; negative indices crash CalibrationMeshView
findings.detail: LandmarkCalibrationStore.save and RegionCalibrationStore.save persist arbitrary Ints with no range validation (/Users/fuannegao/Documents/PERSONAL_WEBSITE/FACEMAP/FaceMap/Analysis/Landmarks/LandmarkCalibrationStore.swift:43-49, RegionCalibrationStore.swift:43-49). Analysis consumers guard both bounds (AnalyzableFace.swift:31-32, SurfaceChangeAnalyzer.swift:59,78, AsymmetryMetric.swift:98, FaceMeshOverlay.swift:240), but CalibrationMeshView guards only the upper bound: `for idx in pickedIndices where idx < verts.count` then `verts[idx]` (/Users/fuannegao/Documents/PERSONAL_WEBSITE/FACEMAP/FaceMap/Visualization/CalibrationMeshView.swift:151-153) and `guard let center = indexLabelCenter, center < verts.count` then `verts[center]` (CalibrationMeshView.swift:171-172). A negative index in UserDefaults (corrupted plist, future bug, or a hand-edited backup) crashes the calibration screen — the very screen needed to fix bad calibration. Also note CalibrationScreen's 'Reset all' clears the persistent store immediately even if the user then backs out without saving (CalibrationScreen.swift:218-223), silently destroying a full 18-landmark calibration.
findings.recommendation: Validate 0..<1220 (or 0..<Int16.max) in both stores' calibrated()/save(), dropping invalid entries; add `idx >= 0` / `center >= 0` to the two CalibrationMeshView guards; gate 'Reset all' behind a confirmation dialog and defer the store clear to Save. Add store tests for negative/huge indices and corrupt Data under the UserDefaults key.

findings.title: Topology-mismatched or undecodable visit comparison silently hides the region-changes section
findings.detail: ComparisonScreen computes regionChanges in .task; if either mesh fails to decode the block is skipped, and SurfaceChangeAnalyzer.regionChanges returns [] on vertex-count mismatch (/Users/fuannegao/Documents/PERSONAL_WEBSITE/FACEMAP/FaceMap/UI/ComparisonScreen.swift:38-43; SurfaceChangeAnalyzer.swift:51). The section renders only `if !regionChanges.isEmpty` (ComparisonScreen.swift:165-167), so the clinician cannot distinguish 'no measurable change' (which gets an explicit noise-floor message) from 'comparison impossible' (nothing at all). Also, region tracking depends on calibrated bony landmarks (SurfaceChangeAnalyzer.swift:41-43 uses FaceLandmarkIndices.vertexIndex, i.e. placeholders until calibrated), yet ComparisonScreen shows no uncalibrated warning banner — unlike AnalysisScreen (AnalysisScreen.swift:100-108) and the PDF.
findings.recommendation: Track a tri-state (loading / computed / incompatible) in ComparisonScreen and render an explicit 'These two captures cannot be compared (mesh missing or topology mismatch)' card for the failure case. Add the CalibrationWarningBanner to ComparisonScreen when LandmarkCalibrationStore.shared.isFullyCalibrated is false, since the stable-landmark offset cancellation rests on those indices.

findings.title: Photo encode failure is silent at capture and in comparison; one missing frontal photo hides the entire before/after row
findings.detail: currentFramePhotoJPEG returns nil on any failure (no currentFrame, CIContext failure, JPEG encode failure — /Users/fuannegao/Documents/PERSONAL_WEBSITE/FACEMAP/FaceMap/Capture/FaceCaptureView.swift:90-95) and the capture flow proceeds without telling the practitioner the clinical photo was not recorded (CaptureScreen.swift:78-81 just stores nil, which removes the dictionary key). Downstream, ComparisonScreen's photo row requires BOTH visits to have a decodable frontal photo and otherwise renders nothing (ComparisonScreen.swift:134-148) — a clinician comparing a legacy (pre-photo) visit against a new one gets no explanation for the missing before/after. The Analysis photos sheet similarly just omits poses (AnalysisScreen.swift:304-317).
findings.recommendation: Surface a non-blocking warning in CaptureScreen when handleSnapshot receives photoJPEG == nil ('Mesh captured; clinical photo failed — retake?'). In ComparisonScreen, show the photo row whenever EITHER visit has a frontal photo, with a 'No photo recorded for this visit' placeholder on the missing side.

findings.title: TreatmentPlanPDF is stale and silently clips: fixed single page, no photos/region-changes/skin-quality, wrong units for two metrics
findings.detail: Known-stale, deepened with specifics: (1) the page is a fixed A4 frame (TreatmentPlanPDF.swift:23,92) — with 8 metric rows plus pins plus long notes the right column overflows and content is clipped with no indication; footer hardcodes 'Page 1 of 1' (/Users/fuannegao/Documents/PERSONAL_WEBSITE/FACEMAP/FaceMap/Reports/TreatmentPlanPDF.swift:270). (2) formatPDFValue's default branch renders SkinQualityAnalyzer and ExpressionAsymmetryMetric values as percentages ('%.1f%% (≤ %.0f%%)', lines 287-297) — a texture index of 0.04 prints as '4.0%', clinically misleading on a signed document (AnalysisScreen.formatValue at AnalysisScreen.swift:536-560 has the correct per-metric formats). (3) No clinical photos, no region-projection changes, no per-pose meshes. (4) exportPDF has three silent-return failure points: nil patient, nil PDF data, temp-file write failure (AnalysisScreen.swift:510-519, ShareSheet.swift:22-31) — the export button can do nothing with zero feedback. (5) The PDF filename embeds the raw label; '/' is sanitized but newlines/control characters are not (ShareSheet.swift:23).
findings.recommendation: Rebuild PDF generation as multi-page (paginate metric rows + notes), port the per-metric formatter from AnalysisScreen into a shared MetricValueFormatter used by both, add a photos page (frontal/oblique L/R) and a region-changes table when a prior visit exists, and alert on export failure. Add a smoke test that generate() returns non-nil for a synthetic case with 10k-char notes and that the uncalibrated strip toggles with store state.

findings.title: Notes are unbounded and saved to SwiftData on every keystroke
findings.detail: AnalysisScreen persists notes via onChange(of: notes) → c.notes = newValue; try? store.context.save() — one full context save per keystroke (/Users/fuannegao/Documents/PERSONAL_WEBSITE/FACEMAP/FaceMap/UI/AnalysisScreen.swift:167-172), again with a silent try?. There is no length cap: a pasted multi-page note (e.g. 100k chars) is re-saved per keystroke, renders fully in the PDF notes block where it silently clips the fixed page (TreatmentPlanPDF.swift:168-177), and the list preview path handles it only because of the 60-char prefix (AnalysisScreen.swift:367-374). PatientCase.notes is a plain String? with no validation (PatientCase.swift:26).
findings.recommendation: Debounce the notes save (e.g. save on sheet dismiss plus a 1-2s debounce), cap notes at a documented length (e.g. 20k chars) with a character counter in the sheet, and truncate in the PDF with an explicit '(continued in app)' marker until pagination lands. Add a persistence test round-tripping a 100k-char notes string.

findings.title: Capture flow has no retake/cancel; post-save re-entry can double-save and overwrites only the last pose
findings.detail: Once a pose auto-captures there is no way to retake it — a blink/grimace frame is locked in for the visit. After the third pose, CaptureScreen stays on the stack with captures intact and currentPose == .obliqueR; navigating back from AnalysisScreen and tapping capture overwrites only obliqueR and re-navigates with a new MultiPoseCapture (/Users/fuannegao/Documents/PERSONAL_WEBSITE/FACEMAP/FaceMap/UI/CaptureScreen.swift:78-96), so a user can save the same visit twice or save a Franken-capture (old frontal + new obliqueR). There is also no explicit way to skip an oblique pose for patients who cannot turn (tap-to-capture mid-turn silently records an off-target yaw with no quality flag).
findings.recommendation: Add a per-pose review step (thumbnail + Retake/Use buttons) or at minimum a 'Start over' button; reset captures/photos/currentPose when returning from a completed flow (.onAppear when multiPoseResult != nil); record the actual yaw at snapshot time on CapturedFace so off-window captures are flaggable later.

findings.title: Frame-averaging buffer assumes a constant vertex count across buffered frames
findings.detail: captureSnapshot takes n = recentGeometries[0].vertices.count and indexes every buffered sample with 0..<n (/Users/fuannegao/Documents/PERSONAL_WEBSITE/FACEMAP/FaceMap/Capture/FaceCaptureView.swift:68-72). ARKit's face mesh topology has been stable, but the code itself documents this as an external invariant; if a session reconfiguration or future ARKit change ever yields a differing count mid-buffer, this is an index-out-of-range crash in the middle of a patient capture. triangleIndices is likewise latched once and never re-validated against later frames (FaceCaptureView.swift:49-51).
findings.recommendation: Filter the buffer at snapshot time to samples whose vertices.count == n (or drop the buffer on count change in didUpdate). One-line defensive guard; cheap insurance for a clinical capture path.

findings.title: Default landmark indices appear to come from a different mesh topology (MediaPipe-style), making uncalibrated output anatomically arbitrary — including in comparison
findings.detail: The placeholder values in FaceLandmarkIndices.defaultVertexIndex (168 nasion, 133/362/33/263 canthi, 234/454 zygions — /Users/fuannegao/Documents/PERSONAL_WEBSITE/FACEMAP/FaceMap/Analysis/Landmarks/FaceLandmarkIndices.swift:35-58) are canonical MediaPipe FaceMesh (468-vertex) indices, not ARKit 1220-vertex topology indices. This deepens the known uncalibrated gap: before calibration the metrics aren't merely imprecise, they measure essentially random points; and SurfaceChangeAnalyzer.stableLandmarks (SurfaceChangeAnalyzer.swift:41-43) consume the same indices, so the bony-landmark offset cancellation in visit comparison is also placeholder-driven — yet ComparisonScreen shows no uncalibrated warning (see related finding). The file header admits 'publicly-circulated reference indices' but the provenance mismatch is worth flagging explicitly in code and in the banner copy.
findings.recommendation: Document in FaceLandmarkIndices that the seeds are MediaPipe-derived and unverified on ARKit topology; strengthen the uncalibrated banner copy to 'values are not anatomically meaningful until calibrated'; after the human performs on-device calibration (deviceTestScript step 5), consider shipping the verified ARKit indices as the new defaults.

findings.title: Test-coverage gaps: persistence, hostile inputs, capture-pose math, and PDF are untested
findings.detail: FaceMapTests/ covers metric math, the two calibration stores' happy paths, surface-change behavior (including topology mismatch — SurfaceChangeAnalyzerTests.swift:90-96), skin quality, and patient migration/photo round-trip. Missing vs. the risk register: (1) CapturedFace Codable round-trip and corrupt-payload decoding (truncated vertexData, short transform, negative triangle indices) — the crash paths in finding 2 have zero coverage; (2) PatientCase decode fallbacks (empty Data, garbage JSON → nil capturedFace / [] metricResults) and the multiPoseCapture reassembly property (PatientCase.swift:103-111); (3) calibration stores with out-of-range/negative indices and corrupt Data under the UserDefaults key (current tests only round-trip valid values — LandmarkCalibrationStoreTests.swift:27, RegionCalibrationStoreTests.swift:27); (4) CapturePose.contains/yawError window math and HeadPose.from(transform:) Euler decomposition (the v0.5 coached-capture core has no tests at all); (5) TreatmentPlanPDF.generate smoke test (non-nil data, uncalibrated strip toggling, long-notes input); (6) noise-floor regression: a test pinning noiseFloorMeters == 0.0003 and exceedsNoiseFloor boundary behavior (RegionChange at exactly 0.3mm); (7) MetricRegistry.evaluateAll on a zero-vertex AnalyzableFace asserting every metric returns its NaN/'Unavailable' failure result rather than trapping; (8) ComparisonScreen delta merging for disjoint metric sets (a visit saved before skin-quality existed vs. one after).
findings.recommendation: Add four test files: CapturedFaceCodableTests (round-trip + 3 corrupt payloads), CapturePoseTests (window boundaries at ±5°, yawError signs, HeadPose decomposition for identity/30°-yaw transforms), CalibrationStoreHostileInputTests (negative/huge indices, corrupt Data), TreatmentPlanPDFTests (smoke + long notes + calibration strip). Extend PatientMigrationTests with empty-Data and garbage-JSON cases and a multiPoseCapture reassembly assertion.

findings.title: Zero-vertex / degenerate face handled correctly in analysis but produces a blank, unexplained viewer
findings.detail: Verified the good news: AnalyzableFace.position bounds-checks (AnalyzableFace.swift:30-34) so metrics on an empty mesh return their 'Unavailable: …' NaN results (e.g. AsymmetryMetric.failure, AsymmetryMetric.swift:105-109), and FaceMeshOverlay.buildMeshEntity guards empty vertices/triangles by returning nil (FaceMeshOverlay.swift:209-211). However the nil path just leaves an empty black viewport with no message — in AnalysisScreen/SummaryHeader/MeshFullScreen the user sees a black box and a wall of 'Unavailable' metrics with no explanation that the capture is degenerate, and Save is still permitted, persisting a useless visit.
findings.recommendation: When face.vertexCount == 0 (or below a sanity threshold like 100), show an explicit 'Capture invalid — please recapture' state in AnalysisScreen and disable Save. Cheap guard at the top of AnalysisScreen.body using face.vertexCount.

audits.deviceTestScript:
# FaceMap v0.5 On-Device Test Script

**Device:** iPhone with TrueDepth (iPhone X or newer), iOS 17+. **Build:** current `main` (5279c57).
**Setup:** Delete any existing FaceMap install first (fresh SwiftData store + fresh UserDefaults = uncalibrated state). Build & run from Xcode. Have good, even lighting and a mirror-height place to sit.

Record PASS/FAIL plus notes for each numbered check. Items marked **[KNOWN-BUG CHECK]** verify findings from this review — expected behavior is described so you can confirm or refute.

---

## 0. First launch & permissions

1. Launch. Confirm the disclaimer gate appears and blocks the app until accepted.
2. Go to the Capture tab. When iOS asks for camera permission, tap **Don't Allow**.
   - **[KNOWN-BUG CHECK]** Expected bug: screen sits on "Position your face in front of the camera" forever with no error or Settings link. Note what you see.
3. Settings → FaceMap → enable Camera. Relaunch the app, return to Capture.

## 1. Three-pose capture with photos

4. **Frontal:** look straight at the camera. Verify: dashed oval goes solid, status pill reads "Hold — capturing…", the ring fills in ~0.6 s, auto-capture fires, progress bar segment "Front" fills.
5. **Oblique L:** follow the prompt "turn your head to your right". Verify the yaw readout (e.g. "Yaw −18°") counts toward −30°, capture auto-fires inside ±5°.
6. **Oblique R:** same to the left (+30°). After the third capture you should land on the Analysis screen automatically.
7. On Analysis: verify the **pose picker** shows Front / ¾ L / ¾ R; switching poses changes the mesh AND re-runs metrics (watch the findings list update).
8. Open **More → Clinical photos**. Verify: 3 pages, one per pose; each photo matches its pose; photos are **unmirrored** (text on a card you hold up should read correctly — this is intentional clinical convention); portrait orientation; acceptable sharpness.
9. Verify the **Skin quality** result appears in the findings (it is photo-derived; it should appear for any pose that has a photo).

## 2. Uncalibrated banner

10. On Analysis (fresh install): verify the **uncalibrated warning banner** appears under the header. Note its wording.
11. Tap the banner — it must open the Calibration screen.

## 3. Calibration with vertex labels

12. In Calibration: rotate/zoom the mesh; try presets (Front, ¾ L, ¾ R, L, R, Top) and the reset-view button.
13. Tap the **"123" button**. Verify cyan vertex-index labels appear clustered around the current landmark's seeded position; tap a vertex and confirm the labels re-center around your tap and the tapped index is shown ("Picked vertex N").
14. Calibrate at least 3 landmarks; use **Undo** and **Skip** once each and confirm sane behavior. Note progress counter ("N of 18 calibrated").
15. Tap **Save**, go back to Analysis → banner should STILL show (partial calibration). 
16. Re-enter calibration — verify it **resumes at the first uncalibrated landmark**. Complete all 18, Save & finish. Banner must now be gone, and metric values should have changed (re-evaluated).
17. **[KNOWN-BUG CHECK]** Re-enter calibration, tap **Reset all**, then back out WITHOUT saving. Re-open Analysis: expected bug — the banner is back because Reset all cleared the persistent store immediately, even though you never tapped Save. Recalibrate fully afterwards (you need this for step 6).
18. **Region painting:** from Calibration tap the paintbrush, paint a couple of regions, save, verify the heatmap/asymmetry visuals use them.

## 4. Save, reopen, persistence

19. From Analysis: add a **note** (a few sentences), then toolbar **Save**, label `P-001 Visit 1`, Save.
20. Go to the Patients tab. **[KNOWN-BUG CHECK]** Expected bug: the visit is under **"Unassigned"**, not a P-001 patient — even if you started capture from inside a patient's detail screen via "Add visit". Verify both entry points (ad-hoc Capture tab AND PatientDetail → Add visit) and record where each visit lands.
21. Open the saved visit. Verify: mesh renders, all 3 poses in the picker, photos sheet shows 3 photos, metric results match what you saved, notes text intact.
22. Force-quit the app (swipe up in app switcher). Relaunch. Reopen the visit — everything must still be there (notes, photos, pins if any).
23. Edit the note to a very long text: paste ~5+ paragraphs. Verify typing stays responsive (each keystroke currently triggers a DB save). Keep this long note for the PDF test.

## 5. Mid-capture interruption

24. Start a new capture. While holding the frontal pose with the ring ~half full, **press the side button** (lock) or swipe to Home. Wait 5 s, return.
   - Verify: no permanently stuck "Capturing…" overlay; tracking resumes.
   - **[KNOWN-BUG CHECK]** If a capture fires immediately on resume, inspect it: it may be averaged from stale pre-interruption frames or captured at the wrong yaw. Note whether the resulting mesh/photo looks wrong.
25. During an oblique hold, have a second person call the phone (or trigger a banner notification + pull down notification center). Same checks.
26. Back out of capture mid-flow (after 1 of 3 poses) via the back button. Re-enter — verify state is sensible (note whether old captures linger in the progress bar).

## 6. Second capture + Compare (noise-floor check)

27. Capture the **same person** again, same lighting, a few minutes later. Neutral face both times. Save as `P-001 Visit 2` (it will land wherever step 20 showed).
28. Open the patient that holds both visits (likely "Unassigned") → menu → **Compare visits** → select both → Compare.
29. Verify the comparison screen top-to-bottom:
   - **Header:** correct day count between visits.
   - **Mesh row:** both meshes render; sync-rotation lock ON: dragging one rotates both; toggle OFF: independent.
   - **CLINICAL PHOTOS row:** both frontal photos side by side with the arrow. (If this row is missing, that's the silent-hide bug — note it.)
   - **REGION PROJECTION CHANGES — the noise-floor check:** since this is the same untreated face captured twice, expected result is the message "No region changed by more than the ±0.3 mm capture-noise floor." If regions DO exceed ±0.3 mm, record which regions and by how much — that is a real signal-quality finding (possibly placeholder stable-landmark indices; you are calibrated, so record values both pre- and post-calibration if possible).
   - **WHEELS + CHANGES table:** every metric shows A → B values with arrows; severities mostly unchanged for a same-face recapture.
30. Compare `Visit 1` against a visit that has **no photos** if you have one (or temporarily test by comparing against an old pre-photo build's record): note whether the photos row vanishes without explanation.

## 7. PDF export

31. Open saved `Visit 1` (must be a saved case — the share icon only appears for saved visits). Tap the share/export icon.
32. Share the PDF to Files and open it. Verify:
   - Header: FACEMAP, practitioner name, patient code, visit label, date.
